An OrderedSet is a custom MutableSet that remembers its order, so that every
entry has an index that can be looked up.
Based on a recipe originally posted to ActiveState Recipes by Raymond Hettiger,
and released under the MIT license.
deque)
MutableSet
Sequencez
3.1c
    Are we being asked to look up a list of things, instead of a single thing?
    We check for the `__iter__` attribute so that this can cover types that
    don't have to be known by this module, such as NumPy arrays.
    Strings, however, should be considered as atomic values to look up, not
    iterables. The same goes for tuples, since they are immutable and therefore
    valid entries.
    We don't need to check for the Python 2 `unicode` type, because it doesn't
    have an `__iter__` attribute anyway.
    
__iter__)
hasattr
isinstance
tuple)
B/usr/lib/python3.9/site-packages/setuptools/_vendor/ordered_set.py
is_iterable

Z!d9d:
OrderedSetz
    An OrderedSet is a custom MutableSet that remembers its order, so that
    every entry has an index that can be looked up.
    Example:
        >>> OrderedSet([1, 1, 2, 3, 2])
        OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
    Nc
items
map)
self
iterabler
__init__4
OrderedSet.__init__c
        Returns the number of unique elements in the ordered set
        Example:
            >>> len(OrderedSet([]))
            0
            >>> len(OrderedSet([1, 2]))
            2
        )
lenr
__len__:
OrderedSet.__len__c
        Get the item at a given index.
        If `index` is a slice, you will get back that slice of items, as a
        new OrderedSet.
        If `index` is a list or a similar iterable, you'll get a list of
        items corresponding to those indices. This is similar to NumPy's
        "fancy indexing". The result is not an OrderedSet because you may ask
        for duplicate indices, and the number of elements returned should be
        the number of elements asked for.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> oset[1]
            2
        c
<listcomp>[
z*OrderedSet.__getitem__.<locals>.<listcomp>
	__index__z+Don't know how to index an OrderedSet by %rN)
slice
	SLICE_ALL

	__class__
	TypeError)
index
resultr
__getitem__F
OrderedSet.__getitem__c
        Return a shallow copy of this object.
        Example:
            >>> this = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> other = this.copy()
            >>> this == other
            True
            >>> this is other
            False
        )
OrderedSet.copyc
__getstate__s
OrderedSet.__getstate__c
stater
__setstate__
OrderedSet.__setstate__c
        Test if the item is in this ordered set
        Example:
            >>> 1 in OrderedSet([1, 3, 2])
            True
            >>> 5 in OrderedSet([1, 3, 2])
            False
        )
keyr
__contains__
OrderedSet.__contains__c
        Add `key` as an item to this OrderedSet, then return its index.
        If `key` is already in the OrderedSet, return the index it already
        had.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet()
            >>> oset.append(3)
            0
            >>> print(oset)
            OrderedSet([3])
        )
appendr)
OrderedSet.addc
        Update the set with the given iterable sequence, then return the index
        of the last element inserted.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> oset.update([3, 1, 5, 1, 4])
            4
            >>> print(oset)
            OrderedSet([1, 2, 3, 5, 4])
        Nz(Argument needs to be an iterable, got %s)
ValueError
type)
sequenceZ
item_index
itemr
update
OrderedSet.updatec
        Get the index of a given entry, raising an IndexError if it's not
        present.
        `key` can be an iterable of entries that is not a string, in which case
        this returns a list of indices.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> oset.index(2)
            1
        c
subkeyr
z$OrderedSet.index.<locals>.<listcomp>)
OrderedSet.indexc
        Remove and return the last element from the set.
        Raises KeyError if the set is empty.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> oset.pop()
            3
        z
Set is empty
KeyErrorr
elemr
OrderedSet.popc
        Remove an element.  Do not raise an exception if absent.
        The MutableSet mixin uses this to implement the .remove() method, which
        *does* raise an error when asked to remove a non-existent item.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> oset.discard(2)
            >>> print(oset)
            OrderedSet([1, 3])
            >>> oset.discard(2)
            >>> print(oset)
            OrderedSet([1, 3])
        
discard
OrderedSet.discardc
        Remove all items from this OrderedSet.
        N)
clearr
OrderedSet.clearc
        Example:
            >>> list(iter(OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])))
            [1, 2, 3]
        )
iterr
OrderedSet.__iter__c
        Example:
            >>> list(reversed(OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])))
            [3, 2, 1]
        )
reversedr
__reversed__
OrderedSet.__reversed__c
%s()z
%s(%r))
__name__r 
__repr__
OrderedSet.__repr__c
        Returns true if the containers have the same items. If `other` is a
        Sequence, then order is checked, otherwise it is ignored.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet([1, 3, 2])
            >>> oset == [1, 3, 2]
            True
            >>> oset == [1, 2, 3]
            False
            >>> oset == [2, 3]
            False
            >>> oset == OrderedSet([3, 2, 1])
            False
        FN)
setr"
otherZ
other_as_setr
__eq__
OrderedSet.__eq__c
        Combines all unique items.
        Each items order is defined by its first appearance.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet.union(OrderedSet([3, 1, 4, 1, 5]), [1, 3], [2, 0])
            >>> print(oset)
            OrderedSet([3, 1, 4, 5, 2, 0])
            >>> oset.union([8, 9])
            OrderedSet([3, 1, 4, 5, 2, 0, 8, 9])
            >>> oset | {10}
            OrderedSet([3, 1, 4, 5, 2, 0, 10])
        )
chain
from_iterable)
sets
clsZ
containersr
union6
OrderedSet.unionc
intersection
__and__I
OrderedSet.__and__c
        Returns elements in common between all sets. Order is defined only
        by the first set.
        Example:
            >>> oset = OrderedSet.intersection(OrderedSet([0, 1, 2, 3]), [1, 2, 3])
            >>> print(oset)
            OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> oset.intersection([2, 4, 5], [1, 2, 3, 4])
            OrderedSet([2])
            >>> oset.intersection()
            OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
        c
commonr
	<genexpr>^
z*OrderedSet.intersection.<locals>.<genexpr>)
OrderedSet.intersectionc
        Returns all elements that are in this set but not the others.
        Example:
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]).difference(OrderedSet([2]))
            OrderedSet([1, 3])
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]).difference(OrderedSet([2]), OrderedSet([3]))
            OrderedSet([1])
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]) - OrderedSet([2])
            OrderedSet([1, 3])
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]).difference()
            OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
        c
z(OrderedSet.difference.<locals>.<genexpr>)
differencec
OrderedSet.differencec
        Report whether another set contains this set.
        Example:
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]).issubset({1, 2})
            False
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]).issubset({1, 2, 3, 4})
            True
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3]).issubset({1, 4, 3, 5})
            False
        Fc
z&OrderedSet.issubset.<locals>.<genexpr>
allrJ
issubsety
OrderedSet.issubsetc
        Report whether this set contains another set.
        Example:
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2]).issuperset([1, 2, 3])
            False
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 2, 3, 4]).issuperset({1, 2, 3})
            True
            >>> OrderedSet([1, 4, 3, 5]).issuperset({1, 2, 3})
            False
        Fc
z(OrderedSet.issuperset.<locals>.<genexpr>rS
issuperset
OrderedSet.issupersetc
        Return the symmetric difference of two OrderedSets as a new set.
        That is, the new set will contain all elements that are in exactly
        one of the sets.
        Their order will be preserved, with elements from `self` preceding
        elements from `other`.
        Example:
            >>> this = OrderedSet([1, 4, 3, 5, 7])
            >>> other = OrderedSet([9, 7, 1, 3, 2])
            >>> this.symmetric_difference(other)
            OrderedSet([4, 5, 9, 2])
        )
diff1Z
diff2r
symmetric_difference
OrderedSet.symmetric_differencec
        Replace the 'items' list of this OrderedSet with a new one, updating
        self.map accordingly.
        c
idxr0
<dictcomp>
z,OrderedSet._update_items.<locals>.<dictcomp>N)
	enumerater
_update_items
OrderedSet._update_itemsc
        Update this OrderedSet to remove items from one or more other sets.
        Example:
            >>> this = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3])
            >>> this.difference_update(OrderedSet([2, 4]))
            >>> print(this)
            OrderedSet([1, 3])
            >>> this = OrderedSet([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
            >>> this.difference_update(OrderedSet([2, 4]), OrderedSet([1, 4, 6]))
            >>> print(this)
            OrderedSet([3, 5])
        c
items_to_remover
z0OrderedSet.difference_update.<locals>.<listcomp>N
difference_update
OrderedSet.difference_updatec
        Update this OrderedSet to keep only items in another set, preserving
        their order in this set.
        Example:
            >>> this = OrderedSet([1, 4, 3, 5, 7])
            >>> other = OrderedSet([9, 7, 1, 3, 2])
            >>> this.intersection_update(other)
            >>> print(this)
            OrderedSet([1, 3, 7])
        c
z2OrderedSet.intersection_update.<locals>.<listcomp>Nr^
intersection_update
OrderedSet.intersection_updatec
        Update this OrderedSet to remove items from another set, then
        add items from the other set that were not present in this set.
        Example:
            >>> this = OrderedSet([1, 4, 3, 5, 7])
            >>> other = OrderedSet([9, 7, 1, 3, 2])
            >>> this.symmetric_difference_update(other)
            >>> print(this)
            OrderedSet([4, 5, 9, 2])
        c
z:OrderedSet.symmetric_difference_update.<locals>.<listcomp>c
items_to_addr
